I now understand the WebAuthn failure. The test IsTrustedOrigin("https://orders.example.com:443") expects true (explicit default port :443 is equivalent to no port), but TrySplitOrigin keeps the :443 literally, so "orders.example.com:443" != "orders.example.com" and it falls through to false.

Let me confirm the exact line numbering for the CS8602 warning, then look at the Dapper failure.


Debugged null-safety issues and verified port normalization logic thoroughly
Debugged null-safety issues and verified port normalization logic thoroughly

Deriving lowercased host:port string from origin Uri directly.

Validating port-stripping logic across diverse test scenarios.

Debugged null-safety issues and verified port normalization logic thoroughly.

Done
Confirmed: line 55 is _publicOriginHostPort = PublicHost.Value.ToLowerInvariant(); — HostString.Value is nullable in this framework version, so .ToLowerInvariant() triggers CS8602.

Now the Dapper failure. Let me look at TableDirectory.cs and find where RestaurantTableSummary is defined:


Diagnosed Dapper constructor mismatch and weighed three resolution strategies
Diagnosed Dapper constructor mismatch and weighed three resolution strategies

Done
The Dapper error is revealing: it wants a constructor with System.DateTime joinsecretrotatedat, System.DateTime createdat — i.e. DateTime, but the record declares DateTimeOffset? / DateTimeOffset. Npgsql is returning DateTime for those columns, and Dapper's constructor matcher can't reconcile the types.

Let me check the migration column types and how other working records in the codebase handle timestamps, so I match the established convention rather than guessing.


Investigated timestamp column mapping across data-access layers
Investigated timestamp column mapping across data-access layers

Investigated timestamp column mapping across data-access layers.

Done
Both join_secret_rotated_at and created_at are timestamptz. Now let me see how the rest of the working data layer reads timestamptz columns — DateTime or DateTimeOffset:


Diagnosed Dapper type conversion discrepancies between class and record patterns
Diagnosed Dapper type conversion discrepancies between class and record patterns

Refactoring approach to eliminate problematic variable persistence.

Diagnosed Dapper type conversion discrepancies between class and record patterns.

Done
My awk leaked the filename across sections, so that survey is unreliable. The key insight from the error, though: RestaurantTableSummary is a positional record (constructor-only), and Dapper's constructor binding is stricter than its property-setter binding — it won't bind a DateTime reader value (what Npgsql returns for timestamptz) to a DateTimeOffset constructor parameter.

Let me verify how PersonDirectory (which reads timestamptz into DateTimeOffset and whose tests pass) does it, so I follow the exact same pattern:


Investigated DateTimeOffset type mismatch causing Dapper binding failures
Investigated DateTimeOffset type mismatch causing Dapper binding failures

Investigated DateTimeOffset type mismatch causing Dapper binding failures.

Done

So either that path has the same latent bug (untested), or there's global Dapper/Npgsql config making DateTimeOffset work. Let me check both: whether DapperPersonDirectory is exercised by any test, and whether there's any global Npgsql/Dapper type configuration.

Done
Two important findings:

DapperPersonDirectory has the identical latent bug — it's a positional record (PersonRow) with DateTimeOffset fields read from timestamptz, but no test exercises it, so it silently passes CI and will throw the same InvalidOperationException the instant the admin People page loads at runtime.
No global Dapper/Npgsql type config exists — nothing makes DateTimeOffset↔timestamptz work. So the fix has to be at the mapping site.

Done
Now I fully understand the header bug: .app-header and .app-session are display: flex with no flex-wrap, so on a 375px iPhone SE the brand + name + three links + sign-out button can't shrink or wrap — they force the header wider than the viewport and push off to the right. The body wraps and stays within the viewport, so only the header overflows. There are no width breakpoints anywhere, so nothing rescues it on small screens.

Both hypotheses confirmed:

AdministrationHome.razor injects IPersonDirectory and calls await People.ListPeopleAsync() on load → the People page 500s on the PersonSummary materialization bug. That's the post-setup 500 you saw (Home doesn't touch the directory, which is why the refresh "worked").
The table pages consume RestaurantTableSummary.CreatedAt/.JoinSecretRotatedAt as DateTimeOffset, so my fix must keep the public records as DateTimeOffset — only the internal Dapper read type changes.

All edits are coherent (note app.css already used flex-wrap: wrap elsewhere, so mine match house style). Let me verify the failing TableAdministrationTests assertions so my UTC conversion round-trips to exactly what they expect:

Line 167 asserts on a raw DB row (it has .JoinSecret, which the summary omits), so that's a test-owned helper type, not mine. The two failing tests are CreateTableAsync_...ReadsItBack and ListTablesAsync_...OldestFirst. Let me read their exact bodies to be sure my conversion satisfies every assertion (not just that materialization stops throwing):


Diagnosed Dapper constructor mismatch; retyping fields resolves materialization issue
Diagnosed Dapper constructor mismatch; retyping fields resolves materialization issue

Done
My fix satisfies both failing tests exactly:

CreateTableAsync_...ReadsItBack: asserts summary.Label, .IsActive, Assert.Null(summary.JoinSecretRotatedAt) (my null branch handles the never-rotated NULL), .TableIdentifier — and does not assert a CreatedAt value. Materialization now succeeds. ✓
ListTablesAsync_...OldestFirst: asserts only label ordering (SQL ORDER BY created_at, untouched). ✓
The stack trace confirms the model precisely: GetTableAsync failed on a freshly-created table where join_secret_rotated_at was NULL — so the trigger is the reader's field type (DateTime for the non-null created_at timestamptz), not the value. Retyping both row members to DateTime lets Dapper's constructor binding match. Let me do one final confirmation that the test's own TableProbeRow isn't a positional DateTimeOffset record (which would corroborate the theory):


Diagnosed binding mechanism discrepancy and packaged corrective files
Diagnosed binding mechanism discrepancy and packaged corrective files

Confirming the test's TableProbeRow shape
Diagnosing property binding versus constructor binding discrepancies.

Diagnosed binding mechanism discrepancy and packaged corrective files.

Done
Line 281 confirms it: TableProbeRow is a class (property binding, which tolerates DateTime→DateTimeOffset), whereas the broken types are positional records (constructor binding, which does not). Model confirmed; the fix is correct.

Now packaging the tar.gz with repo-relative paths so it extracts cleanly at your repo root:


Weighing verbosity against comprehensiveness for code delivery
Weighing verbosity against comprehensiveness for code delivery

Building the tar.gz with the 4 changed files at repo-relative paths
Weighing verbosity against comprehensiveness for code delivery.


Presented file
Done
Congratulations on getting the passkeys registered and becoming the administrator — that's the whole /setup bootstrap working end to end.

Recommendation: fix the current stuff, don't start the next M3 slice
Every fix below is small individually, but together they get you from red to green with zero warnings and kill two visible bugs you actually hit. Starting the next M3 slice tonight would pile more unbuilt code on top — exactly the "more crap" you want to avoid at end of day. So I stopped at a clean, working tree. The next step (M3 slice 2: join-secret rotation + token generate/validate + metrics) is best started fresh tomorrow on green.

The archive above extracts at your repo root and drops all four files into place. No files to delete. No new packages. No new files — all four are edits to existing files.

What was broken and what I changed
1. WebAuthnOriginPolicy.cs — the failing WebAuthn test + the CS8602 warning (this is the F-06a slice you just built for the first time)

The test IsTrustedOrigin("https://orders.example.com:443") expected true ("explicit default port is equivalent"), but TrySplitOrigin kept the :443 literally, so "orders.example.com:443" != "orders.example.com" and it fell through to false. I added a StripDefaultPort helper that drops :443 for https and :80 for http (IPv6-bracket-aware) before comparison. A non-default port like :8443 is left intact, so the "ported tunnel host doesn't match" cases still return false.
The warning at line 55: HostString.Value is nullable in this framework, so .ToLowerInvariant() tripped CS8602. Changed to PublicHost.Value?.ToLowerInvariant() ?? string.Empty.
2 & 3. TableDirectory.cs and PersonDirectory.cs — the Dapper materialization failures (and the post-setup 500)

Root cause: Npgsql returns a timestamptz column as a UTC DateTime, and Dapper's constructor binding (used for positional records) refuses to feed a DateTime into a DateTimeOffset parameter — so RestaurantTableSummary and PersonSummary/PersonRow can't be built. The stack trace proved it's the field type, not the value: GetTableAsync failed on a freshly-created table whose join_secret_rotated_at was NULL — it was created_at (non-null timestamptz) that broke the match. (The test's own TableProbeRow is a class, so property binding tolerates the conversion — which is why it materialized fine and the failure landed later, in the record.)

The important knock-on: AdministrationHome.razor calls IPersonDirectory.ListPeopleAsync() on load, and the setup success screen links to /administration. So PersonSummary has the identical defect — that is almost certainly your 500 right after setup. You then went to Home (which never touches the directory), so the refresh "worked." PersonDirectory has no test, so CI never caught it; only the Tables path is tested.

The fix keeps the public records (RestaurantTableSummary, PersonSummary) as DateTimeOffset so every Razor consumer (.CreatedAt.ToLocalTime(), the lockout comparisons, etc.) is untouched. I read into an internal row whose two timestamps are DateTime/DateTime? (matching the reader exactly), then project to the public record via new DateTimeOffset(DateTime.SpecifyKind(dt, DateTimeKind.Utc)). PersonDirectory already had a PersonRow → ToSummary split, so it was a two-line retype plus the conversion; TableDirectory gained a small RestaurantTableRow + projection mirroring it.

I chose this localized approach over a global Dapper DateTimeOffset type handler on purpose: it touches only the two broken directories and changes nothing about how the (currently green) identity write/read paths behave — lowest blast radius for an end-of-day change. Worth noting for tomorrow: every future M3/M4 positional record that reads a timestamptz into a DateTimeOffset will hit this same wall (sittings, tokens, display devices, orders). If that boilerplate gets tiresome, a single registered type handler is the DRY alternative — but that's a deliberate, test-it-first decision, not an end-of-day one.

4. app.css — the header overflowing to the right on your iPhone SE

.app-header and .app-session were display: flex with no flex-wrap, and there are no width breakpoints anywhere in the stylesheet. On a 375px screen the brand + name + three links + Sign out couldn't shrink or wrap, so they forced the header wider than the viewport while the body wrapped and stayed put — exactly the "header has its own mind about how far right it goes" you described. I added flex-wrap: wrap to both (and a small row-gap on the header) so the session controls drop onto a second line and everything stays within the screen. It's fluid, not a magic breakpoint, which matches how the rest of this stylesheet is built.

After you extract and build
dotnet test should be green (0 failed) with 0 warnings, and the /administration People list should load instead of 500-ing. If the header still looks off after deploying, it's worth a hard-refresh — app.css gets cached, and the container serves a fresh copy on rebuild.

One caveat on the 500: I'm confident about the PersonSummary defect because the People page provably calls it, but I can't prove it was your specific 500 without the server log. If you still see a 500 anywhere after this build, grab the container log around that request (podman logs myrestaurant_web_1) and I'll trace it.


Myrestaurant fixes.tar
GZ 

Want to be notified when Claude responds?






Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.




86
91
